title: Myths in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness Myths in the Way of Objectives Effectiveness   1. I don’t deserve to get what I want or need.

pages.text:
119
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 2a   (I. E. Worksheet 2)
Myths in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness
Myths in the Way of Objectives Effectiveness

   1.	 I don’t deserve to get what I want or need.

   2.	If I make a request, this will show that I am a very weak person.

   3.	I have to know whether a person is going to say yes before I make a request.

   4.	If I ask for something or say no, I can’t stand it if someone gets upset with me.

   5.	If they say no, it will kill me.

   6.	Making requests is a really pushy (bad, self-centered, selfish, etc.) thing to do.

   7.	 Saying no to a request is always a selfish thing to do.

   8.	I should be willing to sacrifice my own needs for others.

   9.	I must be really inadequate if I can’t fix this myself.

 10.	 Obviously, the problem is just in my head. If I would just think differently I wouldn’t have to
bother everybody else.

 11.	 If I don’t have what I want or need, it doesn’t make any difference; I don’t care really.

 12.	Skillfulness is a sign of weakness.
Other myth:
Other myth:
Myths in the Way of Relationship and Self-Respect Effectiveness

 13.	I shouldn’t have to ask (say no); they should know what I want (and do it).

 14.	 They should have known that their behavior would hurt my feelings; I shouldn’t have to tell
them.

 15.	 I shouldn’t have to negotiate or work at getting what I want.

 16.	Other people should be willing to do more for my needs.

 17.	 Other people should like, approve of, and support me.

 18.	They don’t deserve my being skillful or treating them well.

 19.	 Getting what I want when I want it is most important.

 20.	I shouldn’t be fair, kind, courteous, or respectful if others are not so toward me.

 21.	 Revenge will feel so good; it will be worth any negative consequences.

 22.	Only wimps have values.

 23.	Everybody lies.

 24.	Getting what I want is more important than how I get it; the ends really do justify the means.
Other myth:
Other myth:

Myths in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness
Myths in the Way of Objectives Effectiveness


ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Myths in the Way of Interpersonal Effectiveness Myths in the Way of Objectives Effectiveness   1. I don’t deserve to get what I want or need.
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
